behaviors.description: Log entries with the same trace ID are grouped together regardless of which microservice emitted them.
behaviors.justification: This is pure functional logic: partition a collection of log objects by trace ID field. No external I/O, real network, or OS behavior is involved. A unit test that passes sample log objects to the grouping function and verifies the output groups contain the correct entries is sufficient to catch failures.
behaviors.test_description: Create sample log objects with 3 distinct trace IDs repeated across multiple logs (e.g., traceID_A appears in 5 logs, traceID_B in 4, traceID_C in 3). Call the grouping function and verify the output is a map with exactly 3 keys, each containing only the logs that share that trace ID.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test directly exercises grouping logic without file I/O or streaming; no mocking needed.

behaviors.description: The timeline view orders entries by timestamp, correctly handling clock skew of up to 500ms between services.
behaviors.justification: Timeline ordering is deterministic sorting logic with clock-skew tolerance applied algorithmically. Given logs with timestamps, the ordering algorithm sorts and applies tolerance rules—no real time, external I/O, or concurrency. A unit test with sample logs bearing deliberately skewed timestamps is sufficient.
behaviors.test_description: Create 10 log entries with timestamps spanning 2 seconds, simulating clock skew by assigning 300-500ms offsets to different 'microservice' groups. Call the timeline function, verify output is correctly ordered by timestamp, and that the skew tolerance prevents incorrect reordering or dropped entries.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Pure sorting and tolerance logic, testable in-process with synthetic data.

behaviors.description: A latency spike (p99 above 3x the baseline) is detected and flagged in the anomaly report.
behaviors.justification: Anomaly detection is deterministic statistics: compute p99 latency, compare to 3x baseline threshold, flag if exceeded. No real data source, streaming, or external dependency is required. A unit test with synthetic request latencies (with known baseline and p99 values) can verify detection logic.
behaviors.test_description: Create a dataset of 1000 simulated request latencies with baseline around 50ms. Compute p99 and verify it falls below 3x baseline (i.e., no anomaly). Then add 10 spike entries with latency > 150ms, recalculate p99, and verify the spike is now detected and flagged in the anomaly report.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Pure statistical calculation on synthetic data; no external infrastructure needed.

behaviors.description: Malformed JSON log entries are skipped without crashing the pipeline, and the count of skipped entries is reported.
behaviors.justification: The core failure mode is in the JSON parser: when it encounters malformed input, does it gracefully skip and count correctly? A unit test can directly inject malformed JSON strings and verify the parser doesn't crash, returns correct parsed entries, and reports the correct skip count.
behaviors.test_description: Pass a mix of 5 valid JSON log objects and 3 malformed JSON inputs (e.g., missing closing brace, invalid key format, truncated JSON) to the parser. Verify: (1) the parser returns exactly 5 parsed logs without raising an exception, (2) the skipped-entry counter reports 3, and (3) no partial/corrupted data is included in the output.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Error handling in parser is unit-testable; integration needed only if error handling is delegated to stream/pipeline layer.

behaviors.description: The tool handles a burst of 10,000 log entries per second without dropping entries or falling behind.
behaviors.justification: Throughput under sustained load cannot be tested at unit level. A unit test with 10K in-memory objects would not expose real failure modes: file I/O overhead, buffering bottlenecks, memory pressure, garbage collection pauses, or scheduler latency. The minimum is an integration test with a realistic data source (file, stream, or rate-limited producer) sustaining 10K entries/sec, measuring actual throughput and latency behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Generate a file with 100,000 pre-formatted log entries. Feed them to the tool at a sustained 10K entries/sec rate using a timer-driven producer. Measure: (1) total entries processed, (2) count of dropped or skipped entries, (3) latency per 1K-entry batch. Verify input count equals output count plus skipped count, and per-batch latency remains stable (does not grow unboundedly).
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Real I/O and throughput behavior is essential to expose buffering, memory, or processing bottlenecks; in-memory unit tests would pass even if real load fails.
